faq.title: Straight answers
faq.desc: Data, access, pricing, and support. No fine print riddles.
faq.a1: A native iPhone and iPad app that connects to your Google Search Console account. You can review search traffic, queries, page experience, and other property tools without using Safari.
faq.q2: Is there an official Google Search Console app?
faq.a2: No. Google does not ship a Search Console app for iPhone. This is an independent app that uses Google's official Search Console API.
faq.q3: How do I use Search Console on my phone?
faq.a3: Download the app from the App Store and sign in with the Google account that already has access to your properties. You can also open Search Console in Safari, but the app is built for quick mobile reads.
faq.q4: What can I do in the app?
faq.a4: Review performance reports, explore queries and pages, check Core Web Vitals, inspect URLs, manage sitemaps, filter by device or country, pick date ranges, export CSV, download custom PDF reports, and turn on performance alerts.
faq.a5: Tap Sign in with Google and choose the account tied to your properties. The app uses OAuth. Your password is never stored.
faq.a6: Yes. The app talks directly to Google's Search Console API with OAuth 2.0. Your data stays in your Google account. We do not store or sell your Search Console data. You can manage your Google privacy settings here.
faq.privacyLinkText: manage your Google privacy settings here
faq.q7: Is this app affiliated with Google?
faq.a7: No. This is an independent third-party app. It is not made by, affiliated with, endorsed by, or sponsored by Google LLC.
faq.a8: Yes. Cancel anytime in iPhone Settings, your name, Subscriptions. Access continues until the end of the billing period.
faq.q9: Why is the app not free?
faq.a9: The subscription supports ongoing development and API costs. There are no ads and we do not access your data. Try it free for 7 days before subscribing.
faq.q10: Why can't I see my website?
faq.a10: Verify the site in Search Console on the web first. The app only lists properties your Google account can already access. If you just added a site, sign out and back in to refresh the list.
faq.q11: Does the app work on iPad?
faq.a11: Yes. It runs on iPhone and iPad with iOS 18.0 or later and adapts to larger screens.
faq.q12: Can I view Core Web Vitals?
faq.a12: Yes. You can see LCP, CLS, and INP for mobile and desktop from the same property view.
faq.a13: Yes. Every property your account can access in Search Console appears in the app. Switch between them from the main screen.
faq.q14: How often is the data updated?
faq.a14: The app fetches fresh data when you open it or pull to refresh. Google Search Console itself often has a 2 to 3 day processing delay. That limit comes from Google, not the app.
faq.q15: What date ranges can I view?
faq.a15: The same ranges as on the web: 7 days, 28 days, 3 months, 6 months, 12 months, and 16 months.
faq.a16: Not yet. iOS is the focus for now. Sign up for updates if you want to hear about other platforms.
faq.a17: Recent data is cached so you can review it offline. You need a connection to fetch new reports from Google.

about.metaTitle: About | Search Console for iOS (independent app)
about.metaDescription: Why I built an independent iOS app for Google Search Console data. Solo developer, not made by Google.
about.heroTitle:
The app I kept
wishing existed
about.heroSubtitle: Why I built a native Search Console app for iOS, and where it is headed.
about.authorBio: I'm Tarik. I run a few sites and check Search Console on my laptop most days. I wanted that same clarity on iOS. When nothing else matched how I work, I built the app I kept reaching for.
about.founderPhotoAlt: Tarik Zukic, developer of Search Console for iOS
about.storyP1: Search Console became part of my routine. Traffic dips, new queries, a page that suddenly ranks. I checked often enough that opening the desktop site on a phone stopped feeling like a workaround and started feeling like friction.
about.storyP2: Safari on iOS gets the job done if you zoom and wait. I did that for months. Between meetings, on a train, standing in line. Each time I had the same thought: this data matters right now, and the interface was built for a monitor.
about.storyP3: Google publishes an official API. Forum threads go back years asking for a native app. Nothing shipped from Google. Fair enough. They own the web product.
about.storyP4: I tried what was already on the App Store. Some apps asked for more trust than I was willing to give. Others buried the numbers I cared about behind menus and upsells. None felt like opening my own Search Console account.
about.storyHighlight: I wanted one native place for the questions I already ask at my desk: how is traffic, which queries moved, which pages matter, are vitals still healthy.
about.storyP5: So I started with the basics. Sign in with Google. Pick a property. Read performance, queries, top pages, Core Web Vitals, URL inspection, and sitemaps without relearning a new stack. Same account, same API data, less tab hopping.
about.storyP6: The first version was for me. A keyword shift on a slow Tuesday. A vitals warning before a deploy. Proof that a traffic dip was real, not a caching glitch. If the app could answer those quickly, it earned a home screen slot.
about.storyP7: I still use it every day, and I am still improving it. Version 2.0 brought clearer errors, smoother charts, and settings that stay out of the way. Some reports still open the web for now. Your subscription helps me close that gap instead of chasing ads.
about.storyP8: The long-term goal is one spot on my phone for the site information I need, without opening a pile of browser tabs. Search Console for iOS is independent. Not Google. Not an agency pitch deck. One developer, one app, built for people who already trust Search Console and want it within reach on iOS.
about.bridgeToValues: Continue · What I will not compromise on
about.valuePrivacyDesc: The app reads from Google's Search Console API and shows it on your device. I do not store your search data on my servers or sell it. Manage your Google privacy.
about.valueFastDesc: Open, scan, decide. No feed of notifications pretending to be insights. The layout favors the numbers you opened the app for.
about.valueImprovingTitle: Built in the open, one release at a time
about.valueImprovingDesc: I dogfood every build. If a screen feels slow or vague to me, it gets fixed. If something important is still missing, it goes on the list.

releases.metaTitle: Release History | Search Console for iOS
releases.metaDescription: Changelog for the independent Search Console iOS app. Features, fixes, and what's next. Not made by Google.
releases.heroDesc: Track the evolution of Search Console for iOS. Every update brings new features, improvements, and refinements.
releases.changelogLead: What's next first, then every version from launch to today.
releases.nextLead: Ideas I'm weighing for future releases. Nothing here is promised or in a fixed order. I build what helps my workflow and yours.
releases.nextIdea1: Live visitor pulse: can the app show who is on your site right now? Search Console data is delayed, so I'm looking at whether another source or a near-real-time estimate is possible. Not sure yet.
releases.nextIdea2: More native reports: bring indexing, links, and security checks into the app instead of opening Safari for every answer.
releases.nextIdea3: Compare periods: this week vs last week on one screen, without exporting to a spreadsheet first.
releases.nextIdea4: Smarter Overview digests: a short what-changed summary when you open a property.
releases.nextIdea5: Richer widgets: more metrics at a glance on your home screen and Lock Screen.
releases.nextFeedback: Which of these would help you most? Something missing? Send feedback below or use in-app feedback in Settings.

guide.metaTitle: How to Use Google Search Console on iPhone | Independent iOS app guide
guide.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to check Search Console data on iPhone with an independent native app. Not made by Google.
guide.title: How to Use Google Search Console on iPhone
guide.intro: Checking your website's SEO performance on the go shouldn't be hard. This guide explains how to access Google Search Console data directly from your iPhone.
guide.whyTitle: Why Monitor Search Console on Mobile?
guide.whyBody: Website issues can happen anytime. Checking traffic drops or indexing errors from your phone lets you react faster. Whether you are an SEO, a developer, or a site owner, having Search Console data in your pocket saves the back-and-forth to a laptop.
guide.method1Body: Google does not provide an official iOS app for Search Console. However, third-party apps like Search Console for iOS fill this gap by using Google's official API to provide a secure and optimized mobile experience.
guide.benefit1: Native Charts: Smooth, interactive charts optimized for touch screens.
guide.benefit2: Push Notifications: Optional daily, weekly, or monthly reminders to open the app and review performance.
guide.benefit3: Widgets (Pro): Pin clicks, impressions, CTR, and position on your home screen with an active subscription.
guide.benefit4: Multiple properties: Switch between every site your Google account can access in Search Console.
guide.method2Body: You can also access the web version of Google Search Console via Safari or Chrome on your iPhone.
guide.method2Step3: Sign in with your Google account.
guide.method2Note: While this works, the web interface is designed for desktop monitors. Charts may be hard to read, and navigating complex reports on a small screen can be frustrating.
guide.metricsIntro: When checking from your phone, focus on high-level health metrics:
guide.metric1: Total Clicks & Impressions: Is your traffic stable?
guide.metric2: Average Position: Are your key rankings holding steady?
guide.metric3: Core Web Vitals: Run LCP, CLS, and INP checks in the app (Pro), or open the Page Experience report in Search Console on the web for field data.

guideOfficial.metaTitle: Is There an Official Google Search Console App? | iPhone guide
guideOfficial.metaDescription: Google does not publish an official Search Console app for iPhone. Here is what exists instead and how to monitor your site on mobile.
guideOfficial.title: Is There an Official Google Search Console App?
guideOfficial.intro: If you searched for an official Google Search Console app on the App Store, you will not find one. Google publishes Search Console as a web product. There is no first-party iOS or Android app from Google for it.
guideOfficial.pGoogleOffers1: Google Search Console lives at search.google.com/search-console. You sign in with a Google account that has access to your verified properties. The interface works in mobile browsers, but it was built for desktop screens. Tables are wide, charts are dense, and some reports require horizontal scrolling on a phone.
guideOfficial.pGoogleOffers2: Google does offer a Search Console API. That API lets approved apps read performance data, sitemaps, and indexing status for properties you own. The API is the legitimate path third-party developers use to build mobile clients. It is not a workaround. It is how Google expects external tools to integrate.
guideOfficial.h2WhyExpect: Why people expect an official app
guideOfficial.pWhyExpect1: Google ships native apps for Gmail, Drive, Analytics, and Ads. Search Console feels like it should have the same treatment. Site owners check traffic during commutes, after deploys, or when a client asks a quick question. Opening a cramped mobile browser tab is slow compared to a dedicated app with notifications and widgets.
guideOfficial.pWhyExpect2: Google has not announced plans for a native Search Console app. For now, your realistic options are the mobile web, a home-screen shortcut to the web UI, or a third-party app that connects through the official API with your consent.
guideOfficial.pThirdParty1: Several App Store apps label themselves as Search Console clients. Before you install one, check three things: does it use Google's OAuth sign-in (not a random login form), does it request only the scopes it needs, and is the developer transparent about not being Google?
guideOfficial.pThirdParty2: A well-built third-party app should feel native: touch-friendly charts, quick property switching, and optional scheduled push reminders. It should never ask for your Google password outside Google's own sign-in screen.
guideOfficial.pRedFlags1: Skip apps that promise rankings for keywords your site does not rank for yet, or that claim to be "official" without a clear disclaimer. Legitimate clients read data you already own. They do not scrape Google results on your behalf in ways that violate terms of service.
guideOfficial.pRedFlags2: Check the privacy policy before connecting an account. You should see what data is stored on device, what syncs to a server, and how to delete your connection. Revoke OAuth access from myaccount.google.com/permissions if you uninstall.
guideOfficial.h2Choose: How to choose a mobile workflow
guideOfficial.pChoose1: If you only check Search Console once a month, Safari may be enough. Add the site to your home screen for one-tap access. If you monitor multiple properties or react to traffic changes quickly, a native app saves time. You get readable charts, faster navigation, and features like widgets that the web UI does not offer on iOS.
guideOfficial.pChoose2: Whichever path you pick, your Search Console performance data still comes from Google's API. Third-party apps display what that API returns for your account. Property settings, user management, and bulk verification still belong on the web console.
guideOfficial.pChoose3: Bookmark the web property for admin tasks like user management and verification. Use your phone for the daily pulse: traffic direction, indexing surprises, and experience warnings. Splitting roles that way matches how most independent site owners and consultants actually work.

guideMobileOptions.metaTitle: Google Search Console Mobile App Options | iPhone guide
guideMobileOptions.metaDescription: Compare Safari, home-screen shortcuts, and native iOS apps for Google Search Console on iPhone. Pick the mobile workflow that fits your habits.
guideMobileOptions.title: Google Search Console Mobile App Options
guideMobileOptions.intro: You have three realistic paths for a Google Search Console mobile app workflow on iPhone: use Safari, pin the web UI to your home screen, or install a native app that reads your data through Google's API. Each fits a different habit.
guideMobileOptions.h2Option1: Option 1: Mobile web in Safari or Chrome
guideMobileOptions.pOption1a: The simplest approach is to open search.google.com/search-console in your browser and sign in. You get the full product with no install step. This works for occasional checks: verifying a sitemap submitted, glancing at total clicks, or confirming a property is verified.
guideMobileOptions.pOption1b: The tradeoff is usability. Performance reports use wide tables. Comparing date ranges and drilling into queries on a 6-inch screen takes patience. You may need to zoom and scroll horizontally. There are no push notifications when traffic drops.
guideMobileOptions.pOption2a: In Safari, tap Share, then Add to Home Screen. You get an icon that opens Search Console in a standalone browser window. It feels slightly more app-like and saves you from hunting through tabs.
guideMobileOptions.pOption2b: This is still the desktop web UI inside a thin wrapper. You do not gain native charts, widgets, or offline caching of reports. Session timeouts can log you out, which is annoying when you only need a 10-second check before a meeting.
guideMobileOptions.h2Option3: Option 3: Native iOS apps via the API
guideMobileOptions.pOption3a: Native apps connect with Google OAuth and pull Search Console data through the official API. You authorize access per Google account. The app shows performance, indexing, and sitemap data scoped to properties you can already see on the web.
guideMobileOptions.pOption3b: Good native clients optimize for thumb reach: summary cards first, detail on tap, smooth chart panning. Search Console for iOS adds home screen widgets (Pro), scheduled push reminders (daily, weekly, or monthly), and optional performance or Core Web Vitals alert toggles in Settings. Those extras are why many SEOs prefer an app over mobile Safari for daily monitoring.
guideMobileOptions.h2Pick: How to pick the right option
guideMobileOptions.pPick1: Use this quick filter. If you check Search Console less than once a week and mostly need indexing or sitemap status, mobile web or a home screen shortcut is fine. If you track multiple client sites, watch query trends, or need alerts without opening a laptop, a native app saves friction.
guideMobileOptions.pPick2: Security matters regardless of path. Only sign in through Google's OAuth screen. Revoke access from your Google Account permissions page if you stop using an app. Never enter your Google password into a custom form inside a third-party app.
guideMobileOptions.pBreakdown1: Mobile web struggles when you need to compare two date ranges side by side or export a query table. Home screen shortcuts inherit the same limits. Native apps may cache recent reports for faster reopen, but they still depend on API availability and your Google account session.
guideMobileOptions.pBreakdown2: If you manage Search Console for clients, confirm the app supports switching between properties on the same Google account without signing in again. That single feature often decides whether an app sticks on your home screen or gets deleted after a week.
guideMobileOptions.pCannotReplace1: Even the best mobile client is a companion, not a full desktop replacement. Deep URL inspection at volume, bulk export, and some experimental reports are easier on a large screen. Plan to use your phone for monitoring and triage, and your desktop for heavy analysis.

guideCwv.metaTitle: How to Check Core Web Vitals on iPhone | Search Console guide
guideCwv.metaDescription: Check Core Web Vitals on iPhone with Search Console field data in Safari, or run PageSpeed lab analysis in Search Console for iOS (Pro). LCP, INP, and CLS without a laptop.
guideCwv.title: How to Check Core Web Vitals on iPhone
guideCwv.intro: Core Web Vitals measure real user experience: how fast your page loads, how quickly it responds to taps, and how stable the layout is. You can check Core Web Vitals on iPhone through Google Search Console without running lab tools on your Mac.
guideCwv.pThreeMetrics1: Google tracks three Core Web Vitals for page experience signals. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures loading. Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures responsiveness after a user action. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures visual stability while the page loads.
guideCwv.pThreeMetrics2: Search Console shows field data from Chrome users visiting your site. That is real-world performance, not a one-off Lighthouse run on your Wi-Fi. For mobile SEO, pay close attention to the mobile report because many of your visitors load pages on phones like the one in your hand.
guideCwv.h2FindReport: Where to find the report on mobile web
guideCwv.pFindReport1: Open Search Console in Safari, select your property, and go to Experience, then Core Web Vitals. You will see URL groups classified as Good, Needs improvement, or Poor for mobile and desktop. Tap a status bucket to see example URLs and which metric failed.
guideCwv.pFindReport2: The mobile web UI works but the tables are cramped. If you only need a pass or fail answer before a deploy review, that may be enough. If you check vitals weekly across multiple sites, a native app that surfaces the same API data in a phone-first layout saves time.
guideCwv.h2UsingApp: Using Search Console for iOS (PageSpeed lab analysis)
guideCwv.pUsingApp1: Search Console for iOS includes a Core Web Vitals screen that runs PageSpeed Insights on your property URL. You sign in with Google, open a property, and run the analysis to see LCP, CLS, and INP lab scores for mobile and desktop. Results are cached on device for about 24 hours between runs. This feature requires an active Pro subscription (monthly or weekly plan).
guideCwv.pUsingApp2: That is lab data from a fresh PageSpeed run, not the same URL-group field report you see under Experience in Search Console on the web. Use it for quick before-and-after checks on your homepage or a key landing page. For field data across many URLs, open Page Experience from the app's Web Console links or use Safari.
guideCwv.pFieldVsLab1: PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse give lab scores from a simulated load. Search Console field data reflects actual Chrome sessions over the past 28 days. Both are useful. Use field data in Search Console to prioritize URLs affecting real users. Use lab tools on desktop when you need step-by-step diagnostics for a specific page you are fixing.
guideCwv.pFieldVsLab2: Do not panic over a single Poor URL if traffic to that URL is tiny. Focus on templates and URL patterns with high impressions that sit in Needs improvement or Poor. Those are the pages where vitals work moves the needle.
guideCwv.pFixes1: Poor LCP on mobile often traces to hero images without compression, slow server response, or render-blocking scripts. High INP frequently ties to heavy JavaScript on interactive elements. Bad CLS usually means ads, fonts, or images loading without reserved space. You will still fix those on desktop dev tools, but Search Console on iPhone tells you which URL groups deserve that work first.
guideCwv.routineStep1: Once a week, open Core Web Vitals for mobile on your primary property.
guideCwv.routineStep2: Note any URL group that moved from Good to Needs improvement.
guideCwv.routineStep3: Cross-check whether a deploy or plugin update landed in the same window.
guideCwv.routineStep4: Share the affected URL pattern with whoever owns front-end performance.
guideCwv.pRoutineOutro: That four-step loop takes minutes on a phone and keeps small regressions from sitting unnoticed until a client or stakeholder asks why mobile rankings slipped.

guideRankings.metaTitle: How to Track Keyword Rankings from iPhone | Search Console guide
guideRankings.metaDescription: Track keyword rankings from iPhone with Google Search Console performance data. Impressions, clicks, CTR, and average position on mobile.
guideRankings.title: How to Track Keyword Rankings from iPhone
guideRankings.intro: You can track keyword rankings from iPhone using Google Search Console performance data. Search Console does not show a daily rank for every keyword like a rank tracker, but it gives you impressions, clicks, CTR, and average position for queries that actually drove traffic to your site.
guideRankings.h2WhatData: What Search Console ranking data actually is
guideRankings.pWhatData1: Average position in Search Console is an impression-weighted mean for a query or page over the date range you select. If your site appeared at position 3 for half of impressions and position 9 for the other half, the reported average reflects that blend. It is useful for trend watching, not for claiming an exact slot on page one.
guideRankings.pWhatData2: Data is aggregated and can lag by a few days. Privacy thresholds may hide very low-volume queries. That is normal. Focus on queries with enough impressions to show stable patterns week over week.
guideRankings.pOpenPerf1: In Search Console on Safari or in a native iOS app, select your property and open Performance (or Search results). Set the date range to Last 28 days for a balanced view, or compare two periods if you just published new content or changed titles.
guideRankings.pOpenPerf2: Switch the search type filter to Web unless you care about image or video tabs specifically. Most ranking conversations start with standard web results.
guideRankings.h2FilterQueries: Step 2: Filter to the queries you care about
guideRankings.pFilterQueries1: Tap the Queries tab and use the filter box to find brand terms, product names, or topic clusters. Sort by impressions to see what Google already shows often, or sort by position to find queries sitting on page two that might move with small on-page improvements.
guideRankings.pFilterQueries2: Combine query filters with page filters when one URL ranks for many variants. That helps you tie a ranking change to a specific landing page instead of guessing across the whole site.
guideRankings.h2WatchSignals: Step 3: Watch the right signals together
guideRankings.pWatchSignals1: Rankings alone mislead. A query can hold average position while impressions fall because seasonality shifted or a SERP feature took space. Read clicks and CTR alongside position. Rising impressions with flat clicks may mean your snippet needs a better title or description even if average position looks stable.
guideRankings.pWatchSignals2: On iPhone, a native app helps because you can pin a property, open the queries list quickly, and scan charts without pinching a wide table in Safari. You can also turn on scheduled push reminders in Settings so you remember to review totals on a set schedule.
guideRankings.h2MobileWorkflow: Mobile workflow for SEOs and site owners
guideRankings.workflowStep1: Pick 10 to 20 priority queries per property (brand plus money terms).
guideRankings.workflowStep2: Check them every Monday in a 28-day window.
guideRankings.workflowStep3: Log impression or click swings above 20 percent in a shared note.
guideRankings.workflowStep4: When something moves, check indexing and Core Web Vitals before rewriting content.
guideRankings.pWorkflowOutro: That routine fits a phone screen. You are not exporting CSVs on mobile. You are answering one question: did our important queries gain or lose visibility this week?
guideRankings.h2VsTrackers: Search Console vs dedicated rank trackers
guideRankings.pVsTrackers1: Third-party rank trackers scrape or simulate results for arbitrary keywords. Search Console only reports queries where your site earned impressions in Google search. For many businesses that is the dataset that matters because it reflects real visibility, not a synthetic check from one location and device.
guideRankings.pVsTrackers2: Use both if you run competitive campaigns. Use Search Console on iPhone for quick, authoritative checks tied to your verified property. Save heavy competitive tracking for desktop tools when you need city-level SERP screenshots.

blog.lead: In-depth guides grounded in Google Search Console documentation: improve CTR, find pages losing traffic, prioritize Core Web Vitals from field data, and catch indexing issues before they spread. Each article links to official Google help pages.

blogPostCtr.metaTitle: How to Improve Search Click-Through Rate Using Query Data | Search Console blog
blogPostCtr.metaDescription: Use Search Console query data to find high-impression keywords with weak CTR and improve titles and meta descriptions.
blogPostCtr.title: How to Improve Search Click-Through Rate Using Query Data
blogPostCtr.pIntro: Click-through rate (CTR) in Search Console is clicks divided by impressions for a query, page, or other dimension. Google describes CTR as a useful signal for whether users think your result answers their search. Improving snippets on high-impression queries is often faster than chasing ranking changes because you are already visible.
blogPostCtr.h2Performance: What the Performance report actually shows
blogPostCtr.pPerformance1: Open Performance for Search results in Search Console. The default view covers about the last three months, but you can narrow the range (for example, Last 28 days) when you want a recent, stable window. The chart totals clicks, impressions, average CTR, and average position for your property. The table breaks the same metrics down by dimension: Queries, Pages, Countries, Devices, Search appearance, and Dates.
blogPostCtr.pPerformance2: Newer days can show as preliminary data in the chart (Google marks this when you hover). Wait a few days after a title or description change before you judge results, because reporting can lag and early numbers may still move.
blogPostCtr.h2Step1: Step 1: Find queries with impressions but weak CTR
blogPostCtr.pStep1Intro: Google's own workflow for low-CTR pages also applies at the query level:
blogPostCtr.liStep1_1: Select the CTR metric above the chart.
blogPostCtr.liStep1_3: Sort by Impressions so you see terms where Google already shows you often.
blogPostCtr.liStep1_4: Look for queries with meaningful impressions and CTR below your property average.
blogPostCtr.pStep1Outro: Brand queries usually CTR higher than broad informational terms. Prioritize commercial or conversion pages first, but do not ignore informational queries that drive discovery if impressions are large.
blogPostCtr.h2Step2: Step 2: Confirm which URL earns the click
blogPostCtr.pStep2: Click a query row, then open the Pages tab while that query filter is active. You need the URL Google actually ranks for that term. If the wrong URL appears, fix internal linking, consolidate duplicate content, or adjust on-page focus before you rewrite the snippet. Changing a title on page A will not help if Google prefers page B for that query.
blogPostCtr.h2Step3: Step 3: Improve the title link and snippet Google may use
blogPostCtr.pStep3Intro: Google generates title links and snippets automatically. It usually starts from your <title> element and visible page content, and may use your meta description when it better summarizes the page. You cannot force exact wording in live results, but you can follow Google's published best practices:
blogPostCtr.liStep3_1: Title: Unique, descriptive, concise. Avoid keyword stuffing and repeated boilerplate across pages.
blogPostCtr.liStep3_2: Meta description: Accurate summary of that specific page. Google may ignore generic or duplicate descriptions.
blogPostCtr.liStep3_3: On-page heading: Make the main topic obvious so Google does not substitute different text.
blogPostCtr.pStep3Outro: Match language to search intent. If the query is transactional, say what the user gets. If it is informational, state what the page explains. Misleading titles that do not match the page are exactly the kind Google may rewrite.
blogPostCtr.h2Step4: Step 4: Use Search appearance and device filters
blogPostCtr.pStep4: CTR differs by device and result type. Filter by Device to see whether mobile snippets underperform desktop. Use the Search appearance dimension when you have rich results (FAQ, product, and similar). A drop in CTR can reflect a SERP layout change rather than a bad title on the page itself.
blogPostCtr.h2Step5: Step 5: Measure in a clean date window
blogPostCtr.pStep5: After you ship changes, compare a fresh date range against the period before the edit. Use the Date filter's Compare tab if you want side-by-side clicks and CTR in one table. Google notes you can run only one comparison at a time. Focus on impressions and clicks over time, not daily average position noise.
blogPostCtr.h2WontFix: What CTR work will not fix
blogPostCtr.pWontFix: Low CTR with falling impressions usually points to ranking or indexing problems, not snippet copy alone. Very low impression counts can make CTR swing wildly; ignore tail queries until volume is real. And even perfect snippets cannot help if the page is not indexed or not eligible to appear.
blogPostCtr.visualCaptionPerformance: Performance report: sort Queries by Impressions
blogPostCtr.visualMetricsNote: Queries tab · sorted by Impressions · filter queries below your property average CTR
blogPostCtr.visualCaptionQueryPage: Query filter → Pages tab: which URL earns the click
blogPostCtr.visualCaptionSnippet: Title and meta description aligned to query intent
blogPostCtr.visualSnippetDescBefore: Learn about our app. Download today for iOS devices and more features.
blogPostCtr.visualSnippetTitleAfter: Google Search Console on iPhone: Mobile App Options
blogPostCtr.visualSnippetDescAfter: Compare official mobile access vs third-party iOS apps that use Google's Search Console API.

blogPostCtr.source2: How are you performing on Google? - Search Console Help

blogPostTraffic.metaTitle: How to Find Pages Losing Traffic in Search Console | Search Console blog
blogPostTraffic.metaDescription: Compare date ranges in Search Console to spot URLs with falling clicks and impressions before the drop spreads.
blogPostTraffic.title: How to Find Pages Losing Traffic in Search Console
blogPostTraffic.pIntro: Site-wide traffic charts hide which URLs actually changed. The Performance report's Pages dimension lists search clicks and impressions per URL so you can spot losers, winners, and templates worth investigating.
blogPostTraffic.h2Separate: Before you panic: separate property trend from page trend
blogPostTraffic.pSeparate: Total clicks move for seasonality, algorithm updates, tracking changes, and brand demand. Google recommends using dimensions (page, query, country, device) rather than staring only at the property chart. A flat site total can still contain pages down 40% and others up 30%. Your job is to find the URLs that matter to revenue or content goals.
blogPostTraffic.h2Compare: Compare two date ranges the way Google documents it
blogPostTraffic.pCompareIntro: To see what changed period over period:
blogPostTraffic.liCompare_2: Click the Date filter at the top.
blogPostTraffic.liCompare_4: Pick two ranges (for example, last 28 days vs the previous period, or last week vs the week before).
blogPostTraffic.pCompareOutro: The chart and table then show both periods. The table often includes a Difference column for the metric you select. Switch to the Pages tab and sort by click change to surface URLs with the largest drops. Remember: Search Console allows only one comparison at a time. Adding a country comparison replaces a date comparison.
blogPostTraffic.visualCaptionTraffic: Page clicks falling: Compare date ranges
blogPostTraffic.visualCaptionPages: Pages tab sorted by click difference
blogPostTraffic.pReadTogetherIntro: Google groups performance by how users interact with results:
blogPostTraffic.liRead_1: Clicks down, impressions stable: Often snippet, SERP feature, or CTR issue. See our CTR guide before rewriting body content.
blogPostTraffic.liRead_2: Impressions down: Ranking, demand, or indexing may have changed. Check Indexing and URL Inspection next.
blogPostTraffic.liRead_3: Both down: Treat as a broader visibility problem on that URL or template.
blogPostTraffic.visualCaptionDiagnosis: Diagnose the drop before you rewrite content
blogPostTraffic.visualDiagDesc1: Ranking held but fewer people click. Check title, meta description, and rich result changes first.
blogPostTraffic.visualDiagDesc2: Google shows the URL less often. Open Indexing and URL Inspection before a full content rewrite.
blogPostTraffic.visualDiagDesc3: Compare top queries on the URL and check whether a template-wide pattern appears in Pages.
blogPostTraffic.pSlice: Use the Devices and Countries dimensions (or filters) before you rewrite a page. Mobile clicks can fall while desktop holds steady, which points to mobile UX, speed, or mobile-specific ranking rather than the article itself. Country filters help when hreflang, inventory, or local relevance changed for one market.
blogPostTraffic.h2Connect: Connect drops to real site changes
blogPostTraffic.pConnect: Align the drop window with deploy logs, redirect rules, CMS template updates, canonical changes, and content refreshes. If several URLs on the same template fell together, fix the template once. If one URL fell alone, inspect that URL in URL Inspection and compare its top queries in Performance.
blogPostTraffic.h2RuleOut: Rule out indexing before a full rewrite
blogPostTraffic.pRuleOut: A page with zero impressions may simply not be indexed. Google notes that indexed status does not guarantee appearance in every search, but non-indexed URLs will not earn search clicks from Google. For persistent losers, open Indexing > Pages and URL Inspection before you rewrite thousands of words.
blogPostTraffic.pWeekly: Every week, run Compare on the Pages tab for your primary date range. Note URLs with large click losses, check indexing status for the worst offenders, and only then decide between snippet work, technical fixes, or content updates. Export is available from the report if you want to track URLs in a spreadsheet.
blogPostTraffic.source1: Performance report (Search results) - Search Console Help
blogPostTraffic.source2: How are you performing on Google? - Search Console Help
blogPostTraffic.source3: Page indexing report - Search Console Help
blogPostTraffic.relatedCtr: How to Improve Search Click-Through Rate Using Query Data
blogPostTraffic.relatedCwv: How to Prioritize Core Web Vitals Fixes That Actually Matter

blogPostCwv.metaTitle: How to Prioritize Core Web Vitals Fixes That Actually Matter | Search Console blog
blogPostCwv.metaDescription: Use Search Console field data to fix URL groups with the most impressions first, not every Poor URL equally.
blogPostCwv.title: How to Prioritize Core Web Vitals Fixes That Actually Matter
blogPostCwv.pIntro: Core Web Vitals (CWV) measure real-user loading, interactivity, and visual stability. Search Console shows CWV from field data collected through the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX), not from a single lab test. That makes the report the right place to prioritize fixes that affect visitors Google already measured.
blogPostCwv.pWhere1: In Search Console, open Experience > Core Web Vitals. The overview splits data by Mobile and Desktop. Google recommends starting with the platform that drives most of your search traffic, which for many sites is mobile.
blogPostCwv.pWhere2: If you see No data available, the property may be new in Search Console or CrUX may not yet have enough URL-level data for that device type. You can still test individual URLs with PageSpeed Insights or Lighthouse, but those tools do not replace the field-data report.
blogPostCwv.h2Status: How Google assigns Poor, Need improvement, and Good
blogPostCwv.pStatus1: The report groups similar URLs and evaluates three metrics: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), INP (Interaction to Next Paint), and CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift). Google publishes threshold bands. At a high level for each metric: Good meets the fastest band, Need improvement is in the middle band, and Poor exceeds the slowest threshold.
blogPostCwv.pStatus2: A URL group's status equals its worst metric on that device. Example: Good LCP plus Poor CLS is labeled Poor. Only indexed URLs can appear. The report shows a sample of URL groups meant to represent site-wide experience, not every indexed URL.
blogPostCwv.visualCaptionStatus: Core Web Vitals status by metric (mobile field data)
blogPostCwv.visualCaptionThresholds: Google threshold bands: Good, Need improvement, Poor
blogPostCwv.h2Prioritize: Prioritize by traffic, not by audit score alone
blogPostCwv.pPrioritize: Search Console is not designed to look up one arbitrary URL's status quickly. Google directs you to external tests for that. For prioritization, open Poor or Need improvement groups, note the URL pattern (product template, blog layout, homepage), then cross-check those sections in Performance for impressions and clicks. Fix templates with both CWV problems and meaningful search visibility first.
blogPostCwv.visualCaptionPriority: Prioritize URL groups with Poor CWV and high search impressions
blogPostCwv.pTemplatesIntro: URL groups usually share HTML shells, CSS, fonts, tag managers, and hero images. Patching one URL while leaving the template broken wastes time. Typical mobile improvements aligned with Google's metric definitions:
blogPostCwv.liTemplate_1: LCP: Optimize and prioritize the largest above-the-fold image or text block, improve server response time, remove render-blocking resources where safe.
blogPostCwv.liTemplate_2: INP: Reduce long JavaScript tasks on interactive elements such as menus, carts, and filters.
blogPostCwv.liTemplate_3: CLS: Set explicit width and height on images and embeds; avoid inserting content above existing content unless space is reserved.
blogPostCwv.h2Lab: Use lab tools to debug, not to rank priorities
blogPostCwv.pLab: PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse help reproduce issues on a specific URL. Google documents important differences: CrUX groups URLs with parameters; PSI often strips parameters. A URL can look fine in lab data but Poor in field data if real users on slow networks dominate. Use lab tools after you pick a group from Search Console.
blogPostCwv.h2Validate: Validate after deploy on CrUX timelines
blogPostCwv.pValidate: Field data rolls on a 28-day window. After shipping fixes, allow one to two weeks before expecting movement in the report, then confirm the URL group shifts toward Good. Pair with Performance to ensure clicks did not keep falling during the optimization work.
blogPostCwv.source1: Core Web Vitals report - Search Console Help
blogPostCwv.source3: Performance report (Search results) - Search Console Help
blogPostCwv.relatedTraffic: How to Find Pages Losing Traffic in Search Console
blogPostCwv.relatedIndexing: How to Catch Indexing Issues Before They Cost Clicks

blogPostIndexing.metaTitle: How to Catch Indexing Issues Before They Cost Clicks | Search Console blog
blogPostIndexing.metaDescription: Monitor indexing coverage, inspect new URLs, and verify important pages stay eligible after site changes.
blogPostIndexing.title: How to Catch Indexing Issues Before They Cost Clicks
blogPostIndexing.pIntro: Indexing means Google crawled a page, processed it, and stored it in Google's index where it may become eligible for search results and some other surfaces. A URL can lose clicks while your homepage looks healthy if individual URLs drop out of the index or never get indexed after launch.
blogPostIndexing.h2PageReport: Use the Page indexing report for site-wide status
blogPostIndexing.pPageReport1: Open Indexing > Pages in Search Console. The summary chart shows counts of indexed and not-indexed URLs Google knows about for your property. The Why pages aren't indexed table lists reasons (for example, blocked by robots.txt, duplicate without user-selected canonical, crawled - currently not indexed). Click a reason to see example URLs and whether Google considers the source fixable on your site.
blogPostIndexing.pPageReport2: Google is explicit: you should not expect 100% of URLs to be indexed. Alternate or duplicate URLs often should stay out of the index while the canonical version is indexed. Focus on canonical URLs that matter for search.
blogPostIndexing.h2Inspection: Use URL Inspection for one URL at a time
blogPostIndexing.pInspectionIntro: The URL Inspection tool shows Google's indexed version of a specific URL (not a live test unless you run Live Test). It reports:
blogPostIndexing.liInspect_1: Whether the URL is on Google, not on Google, or on Google with issues.
blogPostIndexing.liInspect_4: Last crawl time and referring sitemap, when available.
blogPostIndexing.pInspectionOutro: Important nuance from Google: URL is on Google means the URL is indexed and eligible, but it does not guarantee the URL appears in a given search result. Search results personalize by query, location, and history. After you fix a blocking issue, you can request indexing from the tool; Google enforces a daily inspection quota per property.
blogPostIndexing.visualCaptionIndex: Page indexing report: indexed vs not indexed
blogPostIndexing.visualCaptionInspectOk: URL Inspection: indexed version of one URL
blogPostIndexing.visualInspectCheck1: Page is indexed · Crawl allowed · Indexing allowed
blogPostIndexing.visualInspectCheck3: Last crawl: May 27, 2026 · Referring sitemap detected
blogPostIndexing.visualInspectBlockCheck2: Indexing blocked by noindex tag · Page cannot appear in search results
blogPostIndexing.h2Publish: Inspect new and changed URLs after publish
blogPostIndexing.pPublish: New content can take days to appear in reports. Google suggests sitemaps, internal links, and requesting indexing of important URLs (often starting with the homepage) so crawlers discover pages faster. After launch, run URL Inspection on the live URL to confirm indexing is allowed, fetch succeeded, and the canonical matches your intent.
blogPostIndexing.h2Spikes: Watch for spikes after migrations and template changes
blogPostIndexing.pSpikes: Redirects, HTTPS moves, trailing-slash rules, and CMS changes often produce clusters of duplicates or soft 404s. Verify 301 chains, consistent canonical tags, and that sitemaps list canonical URLs only. Spikes in Duplicate without user-selected canonical or Page with redirect after a deploy are common early warnings.
blogPostIndexing.h2Sitemaps: Check sitemaps when indexed counts drift
blogPostIndexing.pSitemaps: The Sitemaps report shows submitted sitemaps and parsing errors. A stable section of your site should show indexed counts in line with submitted URLs over time. A widening gap after a release may mean Google rejects URLs because of robots rules, noindex tags, or non-canonical URLs in the sitemap file.
blogPostIndexing.visualCaptionSitemap: Sitemaps report: submitted URLs vs indexed from sitemap
blogPostIndexing.visualSitemapGap: 28 URLs in sitemap not indexed — check Page indexing reasons after template or redirect changes.
blogPostIndexing.pChecklistIntro: Within 48 hours of a major release:
blogPostIndexing.liCheck_1: URL Inspection on the homepage and one template URL per major section.
blogPostIndexing.liCheck_2: Review new rows in Why pages aren't indexed.
blogPostIndexing.liCheck_3: Compare Performance impressions on top URLs week over week.
blogPostIndexing.liCheck_4: Validate fixes in Search Console when you resolve a labeled issue type.
blogPostIndexing.pChecklistOutro: Fifteen focused minutes beats guessing from site-wide averages.
blogPostIndexing.source1: Page indexing report - Search Console Help
blogPostIndexing.source2: URL Inspection tool - Search Console Help
blogPostIndexing.relatedTraffic: How to Find Pages Losing Traffic in Search Console
